component_identification.content:
#### Dice
- 2 standard six-sided dice (shared by all players)

#### Realm Cards
- 11 realm cards, each representing a different Stonemaier Games title: Wingspan, Scythe, Viticulture, Euphoria, Charterstone, Between Two Cities, Tapestry, My Little Scythe, Pendulum, Red Rising, and Libertalia
- Each realm card is double-sided with a unique mini-game on each side (standard side and advanced side)

#### Player Sheets
- Dry-erase player sheets (1 per player), each showing 3 realm card slots and a resource tracker
- Alternatively, printed score sheets for single-use play

#### Markers
- Dry-erase markers (1 per player) for writing on the player sheets
- Resource tracking area on each sheet for pumpkins, hearts, and coins

#### Other Components
- Round tracker card
- Rulebook with realm-by-realm reference guide
- Star tracker on each player sheet (cumulative scoring across all 3 rounds)

core_game_loop.content:
#### Game Structure
Rolling Realms plays over exactly **3 rounds**. Each round consists of **3 turns**, for a total of **9 turns** in the game.

#### Round Setup
At the start of each round, deal 3 realm cards face-up to the center of the table. Every player uses the same 3 realms for that round, writing or placing them on their player sheet.

#### Turn Flow (Simultaneous)
1. **Roll dice**: One player rolls the 2 dice. The result is public — every player sees and uses the same two numbers.
2. **Assign dice**: Each player independently assigns one die to one of their 3 active realms and the other die to a different realm. You cannot assign both dice to the same realm. Each realm has its own rules for how the die value is used.
3. **Resolve effects**: Players simultaneously resolve the effects of each assigned die according to that realm's rules. This may generate stars (victory points), resources (pumpkins, hearts, coins), or trigger special abilities.
4. **Track resources**: Update your resource tracker. Pumpkins act as wild resources. Hearts let you adjust a die result by +1 or -1. Coins are used by specific realms as a cost or bonus.

#### Between Rounds
After 3 turns, the round ends. Discard the current 3 realm cards and deal 3 new ones for the next round. Resources (pumpkins, hearts, coins) carry over between rounds. Stars earned are cumulative.

#### Simultaneous Play
Because all players use the same dice results and act simultaneously on their own sheets, player count has no effect on game length. A 6-player game takes the same time as a solo game.

detailed_rules.content:
#### Dice Assignment Rules
- Each turn you must assign exactly one die to one realm and the other die to a different realm. Your third realm receives no die that turn.
- You may choose which die goes to which realm — the assignment is your main decision each turn.
- A realm can receive a die on multiple turns within the same round (it is not locked out after use).

#### Resources

**Stars** — The victory point currency. Stars are tracked cumulatively on your sheet. The player with the most stars after 3 rounds wins.

**Pumpkins** — Wild resources. When a realm requires you to spend a specific resource, you may spend a pumpkin instead. Pumpkins can also substitute for a die assignment in some realms (place a pumpkin instead of a die to use a value of your choice). You may spend 1 pumpkin to use any die face value (1-6) in a realm instead of the actual rolled number.

**Hearts** — Dice modifiers. Spend 1 heart to adjust any single die you are assigning by exactly +1 or -1 (e.g., a rolled 4 can become a 3 or 5). You may spend multiple hearts on the same die to shift it further, but a die can never go below 1 or above 6.

**Coins** — Special currency used by certain realms. Some realms award coins, others require spending coins to activate powerful abilities. Coins have no universal effect — their value depends entirely on the realm that uses them.

#### Realm Card Rules (Representative Examples)

**Viticulture Realm**: Features a row of grape vine values (1-6). When you assign a die here, mark off that number on the vine track. Completing a contiguous sequence of numbers (e.g., 2-3-4) earns stars based on the sequence length. Hearts are especially valuable here for filling gaps.

**Wingspan Realm**: Shows a grid of bird nest eggs. Assign a die to fill a nest matching that value. Completing a full row or column of nests earns bonus stars. Some nests also generate pumpkins or hearts as resources when filled.

**Scythe Realm**: Has a small hex grid representing territories. Assign a die to claim a hex matching that number. Adjacent claimed hexes form regions — larger connected regions score more stars at the end of the round. Strategic placement matters.

**Euphoria Realm**: Uses a pair-matching mechanic. When you assign a die, record its value. If you later assign a die with the same value, you score stars for the match. Higher matched pairs score more stars.

**Charterstone Realm**: Features a building track numbered 1-6. Assign a die to build at that number's position. Each building generates a small ongoing bonus for the rest of the round (resources or star multipliers). Early investment pays off across 3 turns.

**Between Two Cities Realm**: Has a small 3x3 grid. Each die placed fills a square. Scoring is based on completed rows and columns, with bonuses for specific patterns. Both dice values matter for optimal grid placement.

**Tapestry Realm**: Advancement tracks for different civilization aspects. Assign a die to advance on the track matching that value's range. Reaching milestones on tracks earns stars and resources.

#### Advanced Realm Sides
Each realm card has a standard side (used for learning) and an advanced side (flip for experienced players). Advanced sides add complexity — additional scoring conditions, resource interactions, or combo potential. Players may agree to use all standard, all advanced, or a mix.

#### Round Timing
- 3 realm cards are dealt at the start of each round.
- After dealing, players may briefly review the 3 realms before the first die roll.
- After 3 turns (3 die rolls), the round ends. Remove the 3 realm cards and deal 3 new ones.
- In a 3-round game with 11 realm cards, 9 realms are used and 2 are left unused.

#### Solo Mode
For solo play, the rules are identical except you are trying to beat a target score. The rulebook provides difficulty tiers: Peasant (25+ stars), Citizen (35+ stars), Lord (45+ stars), and Ruler (55+ stars).

scoring_and_endgame.content:
#### End of Game
The game ends immediately after the 3rd turn of Round 3 (the 9th turn total). There are no additional end-of-round bonuses beyond the normal realm resolution.

#### Scoring
Stars are the sole victory point currency. Your total star count on your sheet at the end of Round 3 is your final score. Stars come from:

1. **Realm scoring** — Each realm awards stars based on its own rules when you assign dice to it. This is the primary source of points.
2. **Bonus stars from completed patterns** — Some realms grant bonus stars for completing specific patterns (full rows, sequences, sets, or regions) across the 3 turns of a round.
3. **Unused resources do NOT convert to stars** — Leftover pumpkins, hearts, and coins at game end have no value. Spend them during the game.

#### Winning
The player with the most stars wins. In the case of a tie, the player with the most remaining resources (pumpkins + hearts + coins combined) wins. If still tied, the tied players share the victory.

#### Typical Scores
- First-time players: 20-30 stars
- Experienced players: 35-50 stars
- Expert play with advanced realms: 45-60+ stars

#### Score Tracking
Stars are tracked cumulatively on the player sheet. Each time a realm awards stars, add them to your running total. There is no separate round-by-round scoring phase — stars accumulate in real time as dice are resolved.

beginner_strategy.content:
#### Key Principles

- **Read all 3 realms before the first roll.** Understand what each realm rewards before you start assigning dice. Some realms want high numbers, some want low numbers, some want specific values, and some want sequences. Knowing this lets you assign intelligently from turn 1.

- **Spend hearts early, not late.** Hearts let you adjust dice by +1 or -1, which is enormously powerful for filling gaps in sequences (Viticulture) or hitting exact values you need. Don't hoard them for a perfect moment — use them to convert mediocre rolls into productive ones.

- **Pumpkins are your safety net.** A pumpkin lets you place any value (1-6) in a realm. Save pumpkins for the final turn of a round when you know exactly which value will complete a scoring pattern.

- **Prioritize realms with compounding rewards.** Some realms (like Charterstone) give ongoing bonuses that apply to future turns within the round. Assign dice to these realms on turn 1 so the bonus applies to turns 2 and 3. Realms with one-shot scoring can wait.

- **Don't neglect your third realm.** Each turn, one realm gets no die. Rotate which realm sits out so that each realm gets at least 2 dice across the 3 turns. A realm with 0 dice scores nothing — a complete waste.

- **High dice are not always better.** Many realms reward specific values or patterns, not raw numbers. A 2 might be worth more than a 6 in the Viticulture realm if it completes a sequence. Evaluate each die based on what your realms actually need.

- **Plan your round holistically.** With only 3 turns per round, you should mentally sketch a plan for all 3 turns after seeing your realms. Which realm needs 2 dice? Which can score well with just 1? This plan will shift with each roll, but starting with a framework helps.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- Assigning dice purely by highest value without checking what each realm actually scores
- Forgetting to use hearts to adjust dice — they expire at game end with no value
- Saving pumpkins across all 3 rounds and never spending them
- Giving the same realm all the dice while another realm gets nothing and wastes a slot
- Not reading the advanced side of realm cards when playing with experienced groups
- Rushing through turns without considering which realm benefits most from sitting out this turn
